thank you for reporting this and attaching your CMake config. As far as I can see, everything seems to be in order. MedPy has actually been testen with ITK 4.7 (http://pythonhosted.org/MedPy/installation/itkwrapper4.7.html), so I can't find the error immediately. To tell the truth, keeping up with the changes in ITK and the very buggy behaviour of its Python Wrapper is very tedious and I often miss recent changes. I tend to use Convert3D (http://sourceforge.net/p/c3d/git/ci/master/tree/doc/c3d.md) to convert all images to NIfTI format and work with them, which improves stability and avoids loading the very large itk python module.

332,IssueCommentEvent,2017-04-09 09:21:55+00:00,23516699,LEW21/pydbus,9737,LEW21,,,220414943,292774481,"DBus introspection data does not provide information about expected types of values in dictionaries using multiple types inside. So you need to create Variants yourself.
Here's more info about the type descriptions: https://developer.gnome.org/glib/stable/gvariant-format-strings.html

Theoretically pydbus could infer those types automatically based on Python types, but it might lead to problems when talking with a daemon written in a statically-typed language that expects other types than pydbus has inferred.
On the other hand, people usually guess these things, and do not check them in the docs, so automatic guesser might do a better job than they do.",{},,User,False

The part that could be automated though is sth like sampling different training subsets with different columns that don't contain missing values, and dropping all the others. Maybe an approach would be to train models on different training subsets, and then analyzing the results where a minimum number of samples are present, and from there, one can look further which feature columns may be worth imputing at all (vs throwing out potential noise features to shrink the combinatorial search space.

However, I would say that dropping NaN's shouldn't be done in the feature selection algorithm or the learning algorithm itself, but it would be better to write (yet another) wrapper around it. 1017,IssueCommentEvent,2018-02-23 16:55:40+00:00,100879902,Zuzu-Typ/PyGLM,20467351,Zuzu-Typ,,,298354203,368068880,"I'm not sure I get what you're saying.
in the __add__ function of glm::detail::tmat4x4 the temporary variables **o** and **o2** of type _imat4x4_ are used.
The reason for this is so you can use either a matrix, or a matrix-like tuple or list, which cannot be converted to a _tmat4x4*_.

The internal functions for repacking convert a given set of doubles back to a _PyObject*_ (to be Python-compatible). In the process it calls the respective __init__ function with the supplied doubles as the argument.
Originally I thought I would just pack the vectors of the matrix (as in your version) and make the __init__ function replace the matrix' current ones.

The problem with that though would be that if you initialized a matrix with existing vectors, these would be changed if the matrix is changed -- which is not intended (and not the way the actual GLM works).",{},OWNER,User,False

Sorry, I should have clarified the test.  ""Ideal"" is the sine sweep generated natively at the target sampling rate.  The down-sweep at the end is due to aliasing, since the source signal sweeps beyond nyquist at the target sampling rate.
The colorbars are all on the same scale (180dB) to match the SRC test page.
> It looks like your LPF has a stop band rejection of around -100 dB, whereas
> the SRC one is 20-40 dB better.

Yup.  Side note: I thought I was matching SRC's window parameters, but I think this isn't exactly right.  SRC has pre-computed filters stored in header files with comments at the top describing the parameters, but these don't seem to line up to the filter definitions (ie they have different lengths).  I suspect the filters were updated but the comments are stale.  At this point, I'm not really sure where SRC's filters come from, and I'm not particularly interested in matching them exactly, but I would like to reduce the aliasing artifacts in what we have here.

I also looked into [resample](https://github.com/S25RTTR-Aux/s25rttr/blob/master/s-c/resample-1.8.1/src/filterkit.c#L143-L148) but the filter construction code doesn't match the paper; it uses `2 * f_c * sin(f_c * t)/t` rather than `2 * f_c * sinc(f_c * t)`, so there's a missing factor of `f_c` in the divisor.  Otherwise, my window construction should be equivalent (if you supply a kaiser window instead of the default).
> general the way you find an optimal N-point FIR filter to achieve X dB of
> ripple in the passband and Y dB of rejection in the stop band, and with a
> certain width of transition band, is with Remez/Parks-McClelland/iterative

1168,IssueCommentEvent,2018-03-05 19:41:56+00:00,16331291,davisking/dlib,24324908,cchadowitz-pf,,,302419973,370539120,"Sorry, I should have been more clear - the deep learning part of dlib. I wouldn't merge a PR that did this though.  There are a bunch of reasons, but the biggest is because it adds complication to the layer interfaces.  My view of software libraries is basically this: it is the job of the library to provide as simple and easy to use API as possible.  If that means the internal implementation of the library is more complex then so be it.  The layer interface in particular is important because it's the part of the API that users need to understand and replicate to make custom layers.  So it is critically important that it be as simple as possible.  These proposed changes also complicate other parts of the API by adding ""training mode"" switches which have to be set correctly or the program will crash from running out of memory or give the wrong results, depending on the circumstance.

There is also the more general issue of complex compute graphs, not just basic RNN architectures, which something like this wouldn't address.  I do plan to add some support for this stuff into dlib eventually and it will almost certainly have to be a general API for arbitrary computational graphs.  Probably the nodes in the graphs would be defined by the existing compile time tooling.  But how those nodes are connected together is a different question.  Like I said in another post, I'm reasonably confident that a clean and easy to use API can be build around some kind of addition to the tensor object that causes multiple tensors to share the same memory.  Importantly, I don't think that would require modifying any of the layer interfaces.  ",{},,User,False

Just some advice regarding the caching: afaik, the way it should work is that if you get a jwt with a kid that is not cached, you should pull the keys from Google, add all new ones to the cache, and so forth. Aka. the kid is unique for each key. Caching them for an hour might result in you not being able to verify a token for a whole hour if they just released new keys after you cached them. I'm using a simple dictionary for the cache, which is fine just because the kid is unique (e.g. it does not matter if 2 threads set the same kid, it will contain the same key).
Also, your `_get_token_signing_key_id` can be replaced by `jwt.get_unverified_header(id_token)['kid']`. Just to simplify things :)

If the sequence of completion does not matter, ``asyncio.gather`` might be useful:

The diff works great for the ``KeyboardInterrupt`` case, but still ignores a low-level error. 1598,IssueCommentEvent,2016-02-09 12:08:20+00:00,2369386,esheldon/fitsio,348089,taldcroft,,,132289006,181838897,@esheldon - I understand.  I was under the impression here that CFITSIO is doing the heavy lifting so that there shouldn't be such an issue doing the implementation.  Maybe that's naive?

Individual bits in a binary table 'X' or 'B' column may be read/written to/from a *char array by specifying the TBIT datatype. The *char array will be interpreted as an array of logical TRUE (1) or FALSE (0) values that correspond to the value of each bit in the FITS 'X' or 'B' column. Alternatively, the values in a binary table 'X' column may be read/written 8 bits at a time to/from an array of 8-bit integers by specifying the TBYTE datatype.

At first blush this sounds like one just needs to map `<N>X` to an `N`-sized array of `np.byte` (or `np.uint8`?), with `TBIT` datatype, pretty much the same as `D` for doubles.  I guess here there is the decision of whether to use `TBIT` or `TBYTE`.  I think that `astropy.io.fits` just converts each bit to an 8-bit byte when reading.  It actually then writes out as `L` type (one char per bit), so tables don't quite round trip, which is not ideal.
If there is reasonably isolated entry point where one adds support for a new data type I might be able to take a crack at it.",{},,User,False

1745,IssueCommentEvent,2017-05-21 01:18:59+00:00,25129800,mikf/gallery-dl,12064918,Hrxn,,,230165539,302908753,"And links to other reddit posts in that link to other reddit posts? 😉
What I'm trying to say, this alone would already end up in being an endless mess, without going recursively from subreddit to subreddit.
So be careful with that. And in some way, the depth must be limited when doing anything recursively.
So I strongly suggest to start simple and straightforward instead.
But in general, I think supporting reddit is a really good idea.
I'm not really a heavy user of reddit, but I know myself around on that site, so just @-mention me here and I'll help with testing and stuff.
There are fundamentally only two types of posts that can be submitted to reddit:
* Text only (Which is probably not interesting at all for our case here)
* Link/URL referring to an external resource.

But the comment thread that is part of a text only submission can include links to external resources such as direct links to images, etc., obviously. This also applies to the other type of post, of course.
In this second case, the big majority of all subreddits dealing with images features something like this:
* The link post is a direct link to an image hosted on Imgur.
* The picture is hosted by reddit (Domain: i.redd.it). Not directly linked from the subreddit itself unfortunately.
* The link is to an album on Imgur (http(s)://imgur.com/a/\<ID\>, or imgur.com/gallery/\<ID\>) The ID being a 5-digit alphanumeric code.
* Idiots linking to the Imgur page of a single image when they could use the direct link instead.
Many subreddits restrict hosting to only these domains, so that would be the easy part.
But other subreddits, other rules, so this doesn't apply everywhere.
The other easy variant is a post with a direct link to an image as the URL, just hosted on god knows where. But this is just a simple HTTP GET away, not really a big deal.
Sometimes, this is a direct link to a file hosted on Flickr. (xy.staticflickr.com/?????....) but sometimes it is the photo page on Flickr.

Also very popular on reddit: GIFs (huge surprise). But the real `.gif` image file format is on a steady decline, vastly outnumbered by ""GIFV"" on Imgur and links to Gfycat. In these cases, what we get is actually an MP4 file (or WebM, also available usually).

My point is that supporting all different variants of posts on reddit is a pretty big deal. So we should maybe use this space and opportunity here and now to try to reach a consensus on what would be a viable start, and a constructive way forward from there on. 😄
A link with *more than enough* subreddits of images for the interested:

> Anyway, I vote to keep it as it is (with the simplification proposed above).

560 static int fib_nl_fill_rule(struct sk_buff *skb, struct fib_rule *rule,
561                 u32 pid, u32 seq, int type, int flags,
567     nlh = nlmsg_put(skb, pid, seq, type, sizeof(*frh), flags);

So, if `rule->table` fits in one byte (__u8, see also `include/net/fib_rules.h`), everything is OK. If not — only one byte left in the `frh->table`, and the `frh->table` becomes invalid. To get the correct table value one should use `FRA_TABLE`, not `frh->table`.
Since the library on that level decodes exactly what the kernel provides, you get there 249 (0xf9) from 1017 (0x3f9) and 250 (0xfa) from 1018 (0x3fa).

Strictly speacking, it is not a bug, and I can not even fix the value on the fly — packets should be ready for re-encoding, and if I provide «correct» values in `frh->table`, an occasional packet encoding will fail with a type error.
If you have some idea how to handle the case, pls suggest.",{},,User,False

3. Are you talking about the time it takes on Travis or the overhead on TDF servers?

1. For Travis, downloading LibreOffice does slow down the tests, especially for the old versions (~ 1MB/s instead of 10MB/s). But < 1 minute for current versions and < 5 minutes for old versions is still reasonable, I think.
2. For the servers, here's what @nthiebaud (shm_get on IRC) said:
> based on the activity of the underlying project... 6 downloads per commit will really not be a problem.
> if you limit the version downloaded to not EOL version I'm sure that should not cause to much trouble even with the current travis setup.
> or maybe non-EOL + 1 like the latest 4.3 4.4 and 5.0
> that would be 9 downloads per commit
I believe that if 9 downloads are not an issue, then 17 downloads will be fine too. But if you agree, we can remove some of the old versions that are not in RHEL or Debian stable.

4. Without tests we cannot be confident that all options and filters work. I hope that in the long term it will help avoid issues like #211 and #292. But if the tests start failing for bad reasons and nobody's here to fix them, then remove them. It's only one file (.travis.yml) and one directory (ci/).

Even for routine updates and inserts, I write the updates to a file, bcp them to a temporary table, then update the target table in one transaction.  It's a very roundabout way of doing updates, but if you're doing millions of updates to tables containing hundreds of millions of rows, it's the fastest way. 3195,IssueCommentEvent,2016-07-19 12:43:36+00:00,5806020,rueckstiess/mtools,99221,rueckstiess,,,27843578,233620276,"To execute the tests, you can run `nosetests` in the main mtools directory (install first via pip if not available). You can also run an individual file like so:

which skips the entire mlaunch test suite. Comment that line out in order to run the tests.

A bit more info on mlaunch tests: Some are tagged with decorators, for example `@attr('slow')`. These act like tags and you can exclude them from running via nosetests: http://nose.readthedocs.io/en/latest/plugins/attrib.html
There are also `@timed(###)` which has to finish in a certain time, and `@attr('auth')` which is a tag to indicate the test is using authentication.

Another special test is the one called `test_repeat_all_with_auth`. It basically introspects the file and re-runs all the tests that are not tagged with `@attr('auth')` but enables auth this time. 3223,IssueCommentEvent,2018-07-31 21:53:12+00:00,6794278,scanny/python-pptx,2062718,scanny,,,342873279,409380173,"Okay, so here's the logic that I think makes sense of this behavior. Just to recap, in a chart (of at least certain types) that has only _one_ series, the name of the first series appears as the title of the chart without you actually asking PowerPoint to do that.
Leaving aside the question of whether this behavior is _justified_, the reason why this might be _reasonable_ is that the _title can substitute for the legend in this single-series case_.

Generally, a legend maps a colors or symbol to a series, so you can tell which series is which. In the single-series case, you still need the name (what the values represent) but you no longer need the color-patch because there's only one, or because the series uses a different color for each value (bar, segment) (common with pie and I expect doughnut charts, but also an option with bar charts).

So a useful option in this single-series case is to turn the legend off and let the title explain what the chart is representing. Anyway, opinions could differ on whether that's ""helping"", but at least it makes sense to me now why they might have done this on purpose.",{},OWNER,User,False

`1.` When you want convert Unicode string to bytes string with ASCII only
s_uni = u'\u0430\u0431\u0432' # first three letters of russian alphabet
s_uni.encode('ascii') # UnicodeEncodeError, because their codes a far more than >127
s_uni.encode('unicode_escape').encode('ascii')  # OK, unicode_escape gives us byte string with codes <= 127

`2.` When you want convert Bytes string (which MAY contain bytes with codes > 127, e.g. those strings are returned by various python built-in OS, network etc functions) to bytes string with ASCII only (<= 127)
s_uni = u'\u0430\u0431\u0432' # first three letters of russian alphabet
# cp1251 - 1-byte ANSI encoding of Russian alphabet
s_1251.encode('ascii') # UnicodeDecodeError, because s_1251 byte string has bytes with codes > 127
s_1251.encode('string_escape').encode('ascii') # OK, string_escape gives us byte string with codes <= 127

On Sun, Feb 8, 2015 at 10:53 PM, abarnert <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> BaseRemoteMachine.tempdir runs mktemp -d, with no other parameters.
> With the Todd Miller or GNU coreutils versions of mktemp, this works fine
> (at least under GNU, it uses a template of tmp.XXXXXXXXXX).
> But with most other implementations, it's an error. The traditional
> command (it's not actually standardized, but was added in OpenBSD and
> FreeBSD and copied from there to many other systems) requires a template
> parameter. Most current *BSD versions allow a prefix instead of a template,
> but you still have to specify one or the other.
> This means that if you try to use tempdir on a RemoteMachine that's
> connected to, say, OS X, or FreeBSD, you get a ProcessExecutionError.
> This also means that RPyC ZeroDeploy doesn't work on OS X, FreeBSD, etc.
> Changing the command to explicitly use tmp.XXXXXXXXXX will make it work
> *almost* everywhere, and work exactly the same as it has on almost every
> linux distro (and on Cygwin), which I think is the best option.
> Reply to this email directly or view it on GitHub

The way I've understood macaroon trees is that we should bind in this way:
# Prepare the discharge macaroons for request.
This makes sense to me because discharges are points at which different entities can make assertions. Discharges are encapsulation points, if you will.

If we bind sub-discharges to the ""root"" instead of the ""parent"", then I think we have the situation that the paper warns about where discharges can potentially be used to discharge third party caveats they weren't intended to (discharge2 *should* only be used to discharge the third party caveat in discharge1, but because it has to be given ""unbound"" to the root owner, if intercepted it can be rebound to something other than discharge1).

I don't see any difference in the security guarantees of the model used here, but that could definitely be a reason to change it. Otherwise it seems to me like the way it's currently implemented aligns with the intended use of discharge macaroons.
What do you think? Do you buy this argument?
Also, is there some property of binding all of the discharges to the root that you're relying on for a particular application?
If so, we might actually want both:

,{},OWNER,User,False

4165,IssueCommentEvent,2016-11-01 12:06:36+00:00,69691394,kragniz/python-etcd3,1475424,lavagetto,,,184703145,257550698,"Ok so, I compared the two code bases and the first thing I noticed is that I used the `grpc.beta` implementation of grpc, while you used the standard one. The reason to use the beta implementation is simply that the non-beta one doesn't support per-request timeouts, which IMO are pretty necessary for the library to be used in a production environment.

I took a look at your code and I think the changes to use the beta implementation would be pretty limited, would you be interested in supporting request-specific timeouts? If so, my plan for adding features would be something like what follows:
- Switch to the beta implementation, add support for per-request timeouts
- Add a error handling wrapper for requests
- Add the ability to connect to a different server if the current one fails",{},,User,False

* Hosting the audio file within your Flask application
* Trying to use SSML to play the file in a spoken response.

This should be possible today from my understanding and isn't so much a bug as just a use of Flask (and possible feature enhancement for Flask-Ask). Today, you'd need to do something like the following if you plan on hosting the audio file within the Flask application:
1. Use the [static files](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/quickstart/#static-files) capability of Flask to properly expose the file via the web app.

2. Construct a valid url to the static file for substitution into the Alexa response. See the `url_for()` example in the previous link or the doc for [url_for()](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/api/#flask.url_for)

3. Host your Flask app with HTTPS and an SSL certificate meeting Amazon's guidance for hosted media I shared previously (https://developer.amazon.com/public/solutions/alexa/alexa-skills-kit/docs/providing-home-cards-for-the-amazon-alexa-app#image_hosting).

4. You still might need to enable CORS as instructed by Amazon. You can try without, but I'm not sure which parts of the Amazon infrastructure will be making the request for you audio file. For example, for images I believe the Alexa web and mobile apps themselves fetch the files for rendering in cards, so that might be why they require CORS.",{},,User,False

``refresh`` method is available on all resources and is very useful when you need to re-request something from Redmine which is not available by default (like another version of the wiki page).
The other way you already know, as stated in the docs it is:
In this case you are calling the ``get`` method on the ResourceManager object which requests data directly from Redmine and that is why that works.

5967,IssueCommentEvent,2016-05-23 14:29:10+00:00,12420595,CamDavidsonPilon/lifelines,884032,CamDavidsonPilon,,,156136643,220996383,"Hey there, I'm investigating this, and ty for bring this to my attention. I can offer some advice atm. Your script:

is a bit of a pathological case for the concordance index. In this case, all actual events happen at the same time and all events  have been observed. So the concordance index will say ""hey, when eventA happens _before_ eventB, does that hold true for our predicted data?"" Now, if eventA == eventB for all events, then the initial condition (eventA happens _before_ eventB) is never able to be evaluated. What we get is actually no admissible events to compare (technically speaking, we get a division by 0 error in https://github.com/CamDavidsonPilon/lifelines/blob/98e3923d181891eb147a8c8aaa03fa07f00c0ec6/lifelines/utils/__init__.py#L916).
Returning `nan` is wrong though, I think a ValueError or something should be thrown instead.
Another pathological case is when `n==1` - in this case, there are no possible pairs, so a division by zero also occurs.
For your second comment, can you explain why you think 2.5/3 should be the right answer? ",{},,User,False

6250,IssueCommentEvent,2018-02-09 21:15:25+00:00,76535478,treethought/flask-assistant,15894818,treethought,,,295961823,364569426,Thanks for pointing this out @chaoranxie. The reason for generating _alias_ and _meta_ as the same value is so that the parameter can then be mapped from the webhook request to the function's arguments.
The request delivers the parsed parameters via their _alias_ (_parameter name_ in the console), but the function args represent or are mapped to an Entity ('meta' value).
You could potentially use the mapping
However, I think the real issue is the use of two references to a single parsed parameter from a user's phrase.

In my experience with DialogFlow, it is usually an anti-pattern to use the same entity for multiple parameters (often better to use composite entities). However, this double use of the @gender entity isn't really required for what you are trying to accomplish.

Ideally, you would accept a single `gender` parameter that maps to the @gender entity, and extract the ""original"" value inside your webhook. Unfortunately, there is no helper for doing that currently so you'd have to grab it from the request payload.

6254,IssueCommentEvent,2017-12-19 19:43:44+00:00,68146968,vimalloc/flask-jwt-extended,238933,vimalloc,,,283040992,352865676,That's an interesting idea. It would be more limited in a few use cases, specifically blacklisting. If you could use an access token to create an access token, it could become difficult to actually blacklist a compromised token, as each token can keep generating more and more tokens, compared to a refresh token that can only (in the examples at least) be generated with a valid username and password. But that probably isn't a huge deal, and could be worked around on a per application basis.

Without thinking about it too much, I think this could work. The big issue I see is that I don't want to break compatibility with this extension if this change was introduced. I think this extension is flexible enough that the change could be made without breaking existing code bases. My first though would be to update the `fresh` kwarg to take either a boolean or a datetime value, have the view decorators check the datetime value if `fresh` isn't a boolean value, and in the users codebase they could replace `@jwt_refresh_token_required` with `@jwt_required` and generate new access tokens in that endpoint as needed.
I don't have a lot of time to work on this right now, but if you wanted to give it a shot, I would be happy to look over it and see if it was something that could be merged into this extension.

My error handlers return a flask response, not a python dictionary, which ends up in `default_data` and triggers a `AttributeError: 'Response' object has no attribute 'get'` here:
This in turn causes the original app error handler to be run here
and leads to the following stacktrace:

Funny enough, in python3 the *exact* same sequence happens. By registering our error handlers on the flask-restplus api object, we aren't actually having flask-restplus serve our error handlers. We are just setting up a situation where an exception is raised in the flask-restplus error handling, and that triggers flask-restplus to kick the error back up to the native flask error. 6547,IssueCommentEvent,2015-01-15 15:30:25+00:00,11621778,danialfarid/angular-file-upload,700300,Dr-Steve,,,30769611,70102667,"I hit this issue too, I could not upload files over about 100MB.  My standard use case was 200MB files, so this was naturally frustrating.  After a few days of testing different things, I found that it was using filer reader https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/FileReader
Apparently filereader crashes in chrome only on files greater than about 100 MB.  There were a few old bug reports about it on the goolge lists, but no one seemed to care enough to fix it.

My workaround was to not use filereader but instead directly reference the input DOM element (note that I use that content type because I am using pre-signed urls to amazon S3.  You may need a different content type.  Same with PUT vs POST):

Hopefully this helps someone else out there, it took me days to figure out despite it being a relatively simple fix.  I thought that the issue was .http not supporting this size files but it was filereader.",{},,User,False

The actual mechanism involves handing off to a new temporary GangaObject and then to migrate the information to the object in the repository which is the ```Job``` or other object of interest. I suspect that there could be a lot of duplication depending on object scope but if the transient data is accidentally copied which I suspect it is then this could double the memory usage at least.

My plan is to reduce all of this to be that an object contains all of the information it knows about in the schema within the ```__dict__``` of the class. The custom getter can still intercept when an attribute is not in memory, trigger a load from XML and search again with appropriate errors.

I'm not a fan of objects from within the NodeIndexCache being accessed through the standard getter as this is data which depends on the actual repository in question; in addition to not strictly being knowledge that the class should be expected to have imo. Plus it leads to very confusing errors such as 'Job object has no attribute ""display:backend""' which can be counter intuitive.",{},,User,False

7148,IssueCommentEvent,2016-09-07 12:26:16+00:00,43645564,ganga-devs/ganga,61316,milliams,13271048,ganga-devs,175049076,245264315,"Ok, so @drmarkwslater and I have just finished a very long chat about this and feel we've settled on something consistent, simple and sane. I'll start with some ansatzes:
- `put()` only makes sense when `IGangaFile` is used as an inputfile (or manual remote file manupulation e.g. managing your DIRAC files)
- `get()` only makes sense when `IGangaFile` is used as an outputfile (or manual remote file manupulation)
- `put()` should take all the files it finds in `localDir`/`namePattern` and put them in some remote location (except `LocalFile` which needn't do anything).
We should all agree on this to here and if you don't then let's pause here and sort that out first.

The contention here seems to be that while for `DiracFile`, `MassStorageFile` etc. `get()` and `put()` are (reasonably) well defined they are not for `LocalFile` and in particular, `LocalFile.get()` is lacking some flexability.

As far as I can tell, @rob-c's argument is that a user might set an output file to be `outputfiles = [LocalFile(namePattern='*.root', localDir='/directoryA/')]` and then decide that they instead want to copy those output files to `/directoryB/` (perhaps the user deleted `/directoryA/` in the meantime). This is simply solved by defining `LocalDir.get()` to be:
that is, it fetches the files from the job's outputdir inside the `gangadir`. In effect it's is treating the job's outputdir as the 'remote dir' for the purpose of the `get()`.
None of this requires adding a `localPath` argument to `get()` (for any of the classes) as that can always be fulfilled by the `localDir` schema attribute.
If there is a usecase that this does not fulfil then please explain it.",{},,User,False

7259,IssueCommentEvent,2018-05-31 08:02:46+00:00,81349068,snipsco/snips-nlu,11029979,adrienball,2564618,snipsco,327782203,393445873,Hi @elyase, you are right, it is not well documented at the moment.

The [`IntentClassifierDataAugmentationConfig`](https://github.com/snipsco/snips-nlu/blob/develop/snips_nlu/pipeline/configs/intent_classifier.py#L93) is responsible for configuring how data is augmented before training the intent classifier. More specifically, it contains this attribute:
noise_factor (int, optional): Defines the size of the noise to
generate to train the implicit *None* intent, as a multiplier of
the average size of the other intents. Default is 5.

We then generate `None` utterances, by randomly sampling words from text files such as [this one](https://github.com/snipsco/snips-nlu-resources/blob/master/en/noise.txt). The words in these files have been samples based on their global frequency in the corresponding language. Hence, it approximates the words distribution for the language.
This solution is probably not the best one overall, but it has been good enough so far.

Generating utterances for the `None` intent is inherently tricky as it consists in modelling a very large and not well defined corpus: everything which does not correspond to one of the declared intents.
I'm currently working on improving the documentation, and will include this in it.

@johnsca's solutions to this seems like a good one to me. You could also solve this with a `@when_not('upgrade.series.post')` but then the charm author is basically manually simulating the behavior of @johnsca's solution so his seems better to me.

> I think we would be better off just documenting how to use triggers, and maybe coming up with easier ways to use them, making the flag lifecycle changes moot.

7774,IssueCommentEvent,2015-04-23 22:02:31+00:00,11589713,deis/deis,1821014,krancour,6117407,deis,70508573,95731467,"@bacongobbler Sorry... like I said... a straw man.  Didn't invest time in documentation knowing this was all likely to change.  Here's some micro doc:
Traditional install; works the same as it always has:

(Caveat to the above is that if you have previously run the wizard, then uninstalled, and are now re-installing, the options you selected the first time around will still be respected since they're stored in etcd.)

Re: breaking functions like `start/stopServices()` into separate `getServices()` and `start/stopServices()` functions as you have suggested-- I totally like the idea (it sounds cleaner), but even just looking at existing code in the master branch, wouldn't such a change be complicated by the logic needed to start/stop things in a certain order and wait for certain things?  For example, if starting the whole platform, we couldn't just rip through a list of `[ store-monitor, store-daemon, ..., publisher, router@* ]` and start each in turn.  There is (existing) logic that knows where to wait and what things can be started in parallel.  Same goes for the order in which things comes down.

Maybe `getServices()` could return a two dimensional array?  We could then iterate over _groups_ of things to start / stop / etc., with `wg.Wait()` between groups.  If we did that, we'd have to find some clever way to also maintain the output channel messages about different subsystems.  It would call for a more complex data structure than the two dimensional array.  Something like the following:

Seems doable, but then knowing that the order to stop thing varies from the order in which to start things, requiring us to wait in different points, what we'd end up with is logic that looks almost identical to the existing logic, just in a different method.  So I wonder if we'd just end up with code very similar to what we started with.

I setup a health check on my app which only returns a 200 status code if the database is reachable.

Unfortunately, on Friday evening, the AWS machine running our database had a hardware failure which caused the database to go down for about 20 minutes. In the meantime, the health check started failing and our app was eventually completely removed from the router, making it display an Nginx error page. I have now removed the health check to prevent this sort of situation from happening.

7956,IssueCommentEvent,2017-12-19 14:38:01+00:00,39940111,lyst/lightfm,2392579,maciejkula,10947873,lyst,279234197,352774054,"LightFM has (1) user features and (2) item features, these are assumed to be constant over all interactions of that user and that item.

If you want to have contextual features (time-varying, or specific to a given interaction instance), you could integrate them in either user or item features. This way you would have more than one features row for a given user or item. For example, instead of having one row for item 10: `{'id': 10, 'category': 'foo'}` you could have two rows: `{'id': 10, 'category': 'foo', 'time-of-week': 'friday'}` and `{'id': 10, 'category': 'foo', 'time-of-week': 'tuesday'}`. With a little ingenuity in feature engineering you can make the model very flexible. ",{},COLLABORATOR,User,False

8399,IssueCommentEvent,2017-05-16 01:19:20+00:00,17712127,CoreSecurity/pcapy,3599441,guyharris,6544688,CoreSecurity,213648611,301648109,"If you want libpcap to deliver packets as soon as they arrive, you have to turn on immediate mode, and, in the general case, that means using `pcap_create()` and `pcap_activate()` to open a device on which to capture, with a call to `pcap_set_immediate_mode()` between those two calls.  It can't be done if you're using `pcap_open_live()` to open the device - on Linux, you can't do immediate mode with TPACKET_V3, and changing from TPACKET_V3 to TPACKET_V2 involves almost as much work as closing a device and re-opening it, so we require that immediate mode be specified *before* we set up the TPACKET ring buffer, and there are no arguments to `pcap_open_live()` available to indicate that.

So, if you want an immediate return when a packet arrives, and want to support that in a portable fashion, you'll either need to export `pcap_create()`, `pcap_activate()`, and the routines called between those two calls to Python callers or you'll need to export APIs that use them.

(As for the timeout, libpcap has never guaranteed, in its API contract, that the timeout is guaranteed to trigger before any packets have arrived; whether it triggers before any packets have arrived depends on the OS (libpcap being a wrapper around the very-different capture mechanisms on different OSes) and, in the case of Linux, it also depends on the kernel version.)",{},,User,False

8491,IssueCommentEvent,2016-11-04 17:38:33+00:00,24993138,google/brotli,1151,nemequ,1342004,google,142216764,258498754,"> @nemequ Regarding the pc, does have one or several files change how pkg-config will be used?
(I'm not completely sure what you're asking here.  If I haven't answered below, could you re-phrase it a bit?)
Currently there is one pkg-config file (brotli.pc), which links to all three libraries (common, enc, dec).  So you can do

The most common use case will almost certainly be to link to all three libraries, so I think `brotli` should continue to act as it does.  If @eustas wants to include pkg-config support for linking to the libraries individually, I would suggest 4 files: `brotli.pc`, `brotli-common.pc`, `brotli-encoder.pc`, and `brotli-decoder.pc`.  The encoder and decoder packages would depend on common, and brotli would depend on both encoder and decoder (and common via those module's dependencies on it).  That would allow the existing behavior via the `brotli` module, or you could just use `brotli-encoder` or `brotli-decoder`:
> If so, my guess would be to try and follow @bagder 's files so that people can keep using the same setup.

If you're saying the names should be. *e.g.*, `libbrotlidec.pc` instead of `brotli-decoder.pc`, most pkg-config files don't include the ""lib"" prefix (unless the project name includes it, such as ""libpng"") and try to provide ""pretty"" names instead of perfectly matching the shared library names.  Given that the names @badger suggested haven't been part of Brotli, much less an official release, at this point I'd much rather have something which matches pkg-config conventions than @badger's fork, especially considering how trivial it is to swap out the names.  From a technical perspective, though, the names don't really matter.
> Regarding autotools and autogen, I would say these are not important features given that @eustas decided to use different build systems.

A *lot* of people look for configure/autogen.sh scripts.  It's important enough that there is a configure script in the brotli repository which does nothing other than tell people what build systems are available.  I'm not suggesting a full autotools build system, just replacing that script with [configure-cmake](https://github.com/nemequ/configure-cmake/) to provide a more comfortable experience.  It's an easy way to ""fix"" that part of this issue and make brotli a bit more approachable.  Personally, it doesn't matter to me one way or the other; whatever @eustas wants to do.
Anyways, I just need a decision on what to do, then I can throw together a PR (or, of course, this issue can just be closed if you just want to keep everything as-is).",{},,User,False

8577,IssueCommentEvent,2018-06-07 11:07:29+00:00,84183882,google/sentencepiece,11470708,taku910,1342004,google,324384932,395382241,SentencePiece (spm_(encode|decode)) doesn't use *.vocab file, as the same information is stored in the *.model file.  vocab file is emitted just for reference. .NMT toolkit can load the vocab file to manage vocab. spm_encode can directly generate id sequence which can bypass the vocab management in NMT toolkilt.

One note is that  *vocab file doesn't contain all the unique tokens in the training corpus.  spm_train first makes the valid character set so that the character-level coverage gets 99.95% (this can be configured by --character_coverage=1.0 parameter).  This workaround is introduced for handling CJK languages that have larger character coverage, and may contain many noisy symbols depending on the corpus. The rare characters in the corpus are handled as UNK and not in the .vocab file.",{},COLLABORATOR,User,False
Coverage increased (+0.08%) to 97.444% when pulling **45b4527117c5bf52b9bb14c33de9ec7facae9c93 on sr** into **6445aa2b1724e074e19b27dd33f84de370b781d2 on master**.

The default --mining_sentence_size is smaller, because the mining step is memory-consuming.  In this step, frequent substrings are extracted from the corpus to make seed pieces. The space complexity of mining step is about O(4 * 4 N) bytes where N the number of Unicode characters in the corpus.
As this flag only affects the seed vocab selection, the default setting may work as long as the input is randomly shuffled.  The main spm training uses the entire corpus.
By the way, I will make the hard limit of --mining_sentence_size to be the same as other variables, which should be more consistent.

8734,IssueCommentEvent,2015-11-03 06:05:31+00:00,18188537,dronekit/dronekit-python,12703357,tcr3dr,12973369,dronekit,111753336,153253488,"Our behavior in DKPY1 was to repeatedly download parameters continuously. I am unsure if that is the correct behavior, and this is what we currently implement:

1. In our MAVLink event loop, while the parameters set is not complete, we will first request a full list of parameters. Then given a certain threshold (50 params) after a timeout, we decide whether to request missing ones individually, or en masse by requesting them all again. This behavior is shaped to work well over UDP and is largely unnecessary over TCP.
2. Once the parameters set is full, we issue a `parameters` notification (as of #401) and the `.parameters` property has all of its keys defined.
3. Additional parameter changes are sent without prompting from the autopilot via PARAM_VALUE. There is no need to re-request all parameters, as we are seeing updates.
My understand of this may be flawed! But I think this is how the parameters were intended to be designed: http://qgroundcontrol.org/mavlink/parameter_protocol",{},,User,False

yes, indeed it is possible to save and load the extraction calculators that were used on a dataset. Actually we spend a lot time thinking about how we make that possible.

The solution we came up with is based on the `from_columns()` method of the `FeatureExtractionSettings` class.  This method deduces the feature calculators and their parameters from the feature names in the calculated time series feature matrix.
It is important to not calculate features

# the inantiated Settings object will calculate all features

`</s>` is the end of line token.
The second and third row of the dump of the dictionary correspond to the frequency of the token and whether a given token is a word or a label (which is useful for supervised models).

The rows of the dump of `input` and `output` correspond to the parameters of the model. In your example, the first 8 rows of `input` are the vectors associated to the words, while the remaining 2M rows are used to represent subwords. The rows of `output` are the vectors associated to the context. Usually, when learning unsupervised word representations, these are not kept after training.
Please read the paper ""Enriching word vectors with subword information"" for more information on the model.

9047,IssueCommentEvent,2016-08-30 16:28:42+00:00,648332,assimp/assimp,2093195,ZappoMan,265533,assimp,172177714,243497992,"Although our project doesn't use assimp, we have our own implementation of FBX parsing, I glanced over the assimp code to see if I could make a suggestion on how to support the 7.5 version files.
As far as I can tell from the handful of test files I've seen, the 7.5 version files have 64bit values for the per node: `end_offset`, `prop_count`, and `prop_length`...
I believe that you will want to adjust this file/function: `/code/FBXBinaryTokenizer.cpp`
bool ReadScope(TokenList& output_tokens, const char* input, const char*& cursor, const char* end)
// the first word contains the offset at which this block ends
const uint32_t end_offset = ReadWord(input, cursor, end);
// we may get 0 if reading reached the end of the file -
// fbx files have a mysterious extra footer which I don't know
// how to extract any information from, but at least it always
TokenizeError(""block offset is out of range"",input, cursor);
TokenizeError(""block offset is negative out of range"",input, cursor);
// the second data word contains the number of properties in the scope
const uint32_t prop_count = ReadWord(input, cursor, end);
// the third data word contains the length of the property list
const uint32_t prop_length = ReadWord(input, cursor, end);
Pre-7.5 files will use 32bit values for `end_offset`, `prop_count`, and `prop_length`... and post 7.5 files appear to use 64bit values for those.

We autogenerate pipeline DAG in the code (we call them workflows), so in general there is more than one pipeline (the single-pipeline workflow example, I've posted above, is a special case). When one of the pipeline in a DAG fails, it's actually a critical situation for the whole workflow and all pipelines in the DAG should be aborted (that's why we raise Abort there). Later on, when we display the whole DAG status in our UI (using output generated by get_status_tree()), we would like to show user particular pipeline that has failed - we cannot depend on built-in pipeline status as all pipelines in the DAG have 'aborted' state in case of Abort exception being raised. That's why all our pipelines has ""status"" slot.
It looks like problem is in this line: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-pipelines/blob/master/python/src/pipeline/pipeline.py#L3178
The slots are added to the status tree only for the ""child"" pipelines, i.e. slot is not added for root pipeline. Is there a reason for it?

9516,IssueCommentEvent,2017-04-17 19:25:04+00:00,26876372,dateutil/dateutil,1377457,pganssle,9849410,dateutil,222189115,294566117,"Some commentary from #349 that's actually probably more appropriate here:

>This happens because `dateutil` tries to make some context-dependent guesses about what time zone you mean when the time zone is ambiguous. `GMT` could be `Europe/London` or `UTC`, and apparently it prioritizes `local()` over any other interpretation. > A somewhat related bug is #320. I think both can be fixed with a ""sanity check"" when assigning time zones to parsed dates, where, when you are pulling something that's supposed to be a `tzname()` output, you make sure that that `tzname()` *actually* applies at the time of year you've parsed.
> Currently I think the best workaround would be to replace ""GMT"" with ""UTC"", which is hopefully unambiguous.",{},,User,False

1) I'm increasingly liking the idea of a ParseResult-type class as the object being returned from `parser._parse`.  `parser` can be focused on the actual string processing, while `ParseResult` can focus on what to do with the intermediate representation.
2) The `_res_class`-style hook could also be useful for `_timelex`, since there is currently no nice way to use a subclassed _timelex.

3) Related to the partialdatetime discussed elsewhere: there is nothing requiring parser._parse to only identify YmdHMS.  In particular, I think we should consider recognizing a) nanosecond precision, b) BC/BCE years, and c) quarters.  Most users wouldn't be affected, but those who want access to the extra info could get it via either parser._parse or subclassing ParseResult.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

9520,IssueCommentEvent,2018-08-01 19:55:32+00:00,26876372,dateutil/dateutil,1377457,pganssle,9849410,dateutil,344148499,409701152,"I am now fairly convinced that we cannot (yet) implement `__hash__` on `tzoffset` or `tzutc`. The reason is that they define `__eq__` and [two objects must have the same hash if they are equal](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__hash__):
>The only required property is that objects which compare equal have the same hash value;

This is a problem because python [uses `is` instead of `__eq__`](https://blog.ganssle.io/articles/2018/02/a-curious-case-datetimes.html) when making decisions about [aware datetime semantics](https://blog.ganssle.io/articles/2018/02/aware-datetime-arithmetic.html). As a result, in my opinion time zones are only meaningfully equal if they are the same object, but we define `__eq__` in such a way that `tzutc()` is equal to *any* `tzoffset` object that has no offset, and all `tzoffset` objects (regardless of their `name`), are equal if their offsets are equal. This means things like:

It seems like that's a footgun waiting to happen, if you wanted to, for example, map `tz.tzoffset` objects to `tz.tzfile` objects. Unfortunately, `datetime.timezone` *also* has this perverse behavior for whatever reason. As a result, I'm not sure that hashability would be meaningful or useful in `tz.tzoffset` objects. I'm considering changing how `__eq__` works for the breaking 3.0 change, though the fact that the standard library acts this way as well throws a monkey wrench into things.",{},MEMBER,User,False

So there is likely bugs you are right.
In this particular case, I'm unsure it is an IPython bug as I can reproduce the exact same behavior in a plain Python interpreter.

I must admit that I'm far from an AsyncIO expert, but as far as i understand: `asyncio.wait(tasks)` does not consume the task, it just block until the task is finished, which likely means you get a list of futures:
[<Task finished coro=<anything() done, defined at <ipython-input-1-f8502dfae557>:5> exception=NameError(""name 'err' is not defined"",)>,

If you ask for this task result it should raise (and does raise apparently):
237                 # We use the `send` method directly, because coroutines
----> 9     err                                                    # exceptions seem to be ignored

Which should automatically raise if you use it in a generator or yield the task in a coroutine [but here it's starting to get fuzzy in my brain], and it will raise in the context were you use it, not in the context of where it is defined.

The interpreter that run the notebook is **likely** different than the one that run the **kernel**.

`frameworkpython -m jupyter notebook` is roughly useless as you run the jupyter command with `frameworkpython`, it shells out, so the interpreter of notebook can perfectly be not the framework one (and heck the notebook could be written in go you wouldn't notice) , then it's start the kernel which could again be in another language or interpreter.

to get a new available frameworkpython kernel, or just drop the `--name 'frameworkpython'` to replace the default python kernel. Using `%matplotlib inline`  might also help as then it does not try to use the osx backend.

They're a general concept, but Python provides an implementation in [concurrent.futures](https://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html#module-concurrent.futures). They're normally used in code that's doing more than one thing at once.

I think that's probably more complex than you need, though. A cell that you've started running will keep going when you close the browser tab, but the output it produces is lost. The easiest workaround is just to leave the browser tab open - tabs are cheap, I've got ~50 open now. If you can't do that for some reason, make sure it assigns any results you want to keep to a variable - they should still be available when you open it again. You can also use the [%capture magic](http://ipython.readthedocs.io/en/stable/interactive/magics.html#cellmagic-capture) to store printed output into a variable you can get later.",{},,User,False

9806,IssueCommentEvent,2016-08-23 23:43:31+00:00,24774658,boto/boto3,368057,jamesls,327752,boto,172247522,241916064,"There's potential to improve this, but I wanted to explain the error's your seeing, and at least why it's the current expected behavior.
* Setting a region either in your config file or via the `client()` call is specifying which region to send the request to.

* Specifying which region you want to *create* a bucket in is done via the API call `create_bucket` and specifying the `CreateBucketConfiguration` parameter.  This is the *only* way to specify which region you want to create a bucket.

The improvement we can make is to automatically inject the `CreateBucketConfiguration` parameter on your behalf, with the correct values, if you don't provide one, based on the region you have configured.  That way you'll always create a bucket in whatever region you're client is configured for.

The main area of concern is the fact that this is a change in default behavior and we'd need to make sure no one would be adversely affected. The `error -> now working` case should be fine, but there's concern if anyone is relying on the behavior for creating a bucket in US Standard because they didn't specify a `CreateBucketConfiguration` despite the fact that they might have `us-west-2` configured.
Going to label this as needs-discussion for the time being.",{},,User,False

9819,IssueCommentEvent,2018-07-09 19:59:21+00:00,24774658,boto/boto3,1652600,chisholm,327752,boto,334260805,403601471,"Here's what I think the OS-level underlying problem is.  So this is in software layers beneath Python now.  The OS' `localtime()` function behaves differently on Windows and Linux.  Maybe you couldn't reproduce the error because you weren't trying to reproduce it on Windows?  Microsoft's localtime() [documentation](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/bf12f0hc.aspx) says:
These functions validate their parameters. If timer is a null pointer, or if the timer value
is negative, these functions invoke an invalid parameter handler, as described in Parameter
Validation. If execution is allowed to continue, the functions return NULL and set errno to
I'm guessing `EINVAL`'s value is 22.  I verified the error behavior with the following short C program:

If you compile and run this on Linux (I used a CentOS7 VM) and Windows (again, I am on Windows 10, and I used MinGW-w64 as the compiler since I don't have Visual Studio), you will get different results.  You'll get the ""Success"" message on Linux, and `Got errno: 22` on Windows.
I can verify via my Python debugger, that the code at the bottom frame of the above stack traces:
Is calling time.localtime() with a negative value: `timestamp` equals -21600, and as mentioned above, `time.timezone` for me equals 18000.  Their sum is -3600, which is negative.

dateutil's tzlocal() implementation depends on Python's `time` module, and the behavior of Python's `time` module most likely depends on the underlying OS's behavior with regard to certain time-related functions.  Differences in OS behavior will therefore bubble up to the time module, to the dateutil `tzlocal` implementation, and therefore up to boto3.  So it seems like boto3 is currently unusable with the local Dynamo server on Windows.
Again, seems like the simplest fix is to not try to convert these timestamps to local time.  Just stick with UTC.",{},NONE,User,False

9854,IssueCommentEvent,2016-03-14 10:18:14+00:00,6670942,boto/botocore,9458045,migtor,327752,boto,138133880,196241168,"I think the RFC algorithm and the one proposed here are equivalent if we take into account that we also need to remove consecutive slashes. In this case it makes sense to process the URL path as a list of segments. In my understanding of the RFC, if we consider complete segments and forget about the slashes, the algorithm is just:
- If the segment is `.`, just remove it.
- If the segment is `..`, remove it and remove the previous segment as well (if any).

The paths can be seen as Unix directory paths, so for example `/home/./user1/../user2` becomes `/home/user2`, that's what the algorithm is doing, and what I understand the RFC is trying to do as well. The RFC requires this because the dots are only present in relative references, the absolute URL path should never have these dots, so they are 'resolved'.
IMHO the RFC code is harder to understand, but makes sense for example for C implementations of the algorithm, which is usually the case in the examples given by RFCs.",{},,User,False

9959,IssueCommentEvent,2018-01-25 12:17:39+00:00,93721432,awslabs/sockeye,2027990,fhieber,3299148,awslabs,289695479,360450410,"Thanks everyone already for all the great inputs. It's good that we have this discussion jointly!

We should also not overengineer this solution right from the beginning and support all kinds of variations. That said, I think that reading factors from multiple input files seems easier as one can reuse the existing `SequenceReader` class with the corresponding vocabulary. If we allow token-level annotation (with any separator string), we'd need to revise a lot more code in data_io.py, which is not impossible but makes this change even larger than it is already. So my suggestion would be to go for separate files for the _training_ part.

I actually like the concept that Michael brought up for considering the surface form of the source as the first/primary factor. It's a nice abstraction. So I would also be ok with `--source` accepting the factor files, even when this means that `--num-embed` requires some more parsing logic. We can add another parse function to arguments.py that returns a custom object holding embedding sizes for source, factors, and target maybe.

For _inference_ from the CLI, I am also inclined to follow Michael's arguments, but would still suggest that we implement it in a way such that we can change the separator string if needed. We can define the default globally in constants.py and make the parsing logic independent of the actual string. As said before by multiple people, the library use case should not be concerned with serializing metadata/factors into a single string but should be able to use a data structure for this.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

10133,IssueCommentEvent,2016-03-24 15:28:34+00:00,32944298,Z3Prover/z3,3085284,NikolajBjorner,11668813,Z3Prover,143275230,200886459,It's a feature, but I think I see the inconsistency: models given by lookup-tables may have undetermined default cases when model completion is off and it may have resulted in null in a distant past.

The behavior is that model completion=false is that sub-expressions are only converted into values when the current model specifies a value. Otherwise, sub-expressions are retained. Model evaluation in both cases performs rewriting to do constant folding if the expression you are evaluating corresponds to a compound expression.

Models expose tables for constants and functions. You can query these tables to determine if a constant is a don't care. You can use the function tables to also evaluate (on your own) if a function application is handled by the current cases.

This also made me realize that the bindings are also made local to a `.so` file not a `py::module` which adds some nuance: all modules created with `def_submodule` would share the same local bindings, as would all embedded modules.

10211,IssueCommentEvent,2017-03-13 15:49:43+00:00,38581626,pybind/pybind11,4831417,dean0x7d,17565521,pybind,207839655,286149403,"The performance hit that you're seeing is due to `py::vectorize` creating a copy of the non-unit stride array (it converts it to unit stride before executing the function). It should be possible to resolve this by taking non-unit strides into account natively, but I'm not too familiar with `py::vectorize` (I don't use it myself) so I'd rather not muck around in the code. I think for example, that adding more INTERFACE compile flags is contra-productive: I want to test my binding code for -Wshadow, -pedantic or anything exotic that one experiments with. Disabling those on whole translation units just because I add pybind11 headers will prevent downstream users to have simply different policies (on warnings) than their dependencies.

It isn't disabled on the whole translation unit, but merely within the pybind headers (via a `#pragma GCC diagnostic push`/`pop` or equivalent for other compilers) for known compiler false-positives within pybind code. 10522,IssueCommentEvent,2017-12-21 06:10:38+00:00,79083614,python-trio/trio,609896,njsmith,26335827,python-trio,283777031,353266832,Hey, thanks for your interest! And those are really interesting measurements...
There is one complication: we don't *quite* use `sortedcontainers` just as a pure priority queue. We need three operations:
* delete an arbitrary item (for when a deadline gets removed)

`heapq` provides fast versions of the first two operations, but not the third, so it's not just a trivial drop-in replacement. Basically I'm using `sortedcollections` because it was a quick and easy way to get reasonable behavior (and in particular worst-case behavior) for all of these :-). If there's something else that's faster and has reasonable worst-case behavior then that'd be great though.

There are *tons* of possible options. The simplest thing is to use a heapq and leave stale items in the heap, then drop them as they come out – but this makes me nervous because if you have a workload that frequently creates and then removes deadlines, then it you can end up wasting a lot of memory just holding onto stale deadlines. You can also go all the way to proper timer wheels – curio has one of these – but I'm not sure how fast they end up being once implemented in Python. Or double-linked skiplists. Or... [here's an interesting summary of an article from 1987 going over lots of options](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/pull/384) :-).
How does your version work? Do you think there's a practical improvement that's worth doing?",{},OWNER,User,False

2. There are already other [bitly libraries for python](https://github.com/bitly/bitly-api-python) out there with better test coverage and more use in the wild. This code introduces a very tight coupling between the imgix service and the bit.ly service where previously none existed.

10726,IssueCommentEvent,2017-11-23 00:36:46+00:00,13258039,aio-libs/aiohttp,931051,FichteFoll,7049303,aio-libs,275926614,346509197,"Regarding the header stuff, I think I can implement byte string headers and automatic conversion using `ascii` (and not `utf-8`!) reasonably by converting unicode strings in Payload's `__init__`, if necessary, and then changing `MultipartWriter.append_payload` to expect the headers to be byte strings already. I haven't looked at where else I would need to implement this especially regarding the main request/response's and would rather not persue that within this PR, but Payload seems to only be used by multipart related code (and its headers property is only accessed within MultipartWriter).
Regarding the motivation of that suggested change:

To my knowledge, the entire HEAD part (also in multipart) of HTTP is byte-based and only characters in the ASCII range are used by indicators (i.e. header names, values in the IANA encoding registry, etc.) while other bytes >=0x80 may be used in some locations, for example in the boundary parameter here. A such, I believe this internal representation should be reached as early as possible and only public API should accept unicode strings in the ASCII range because those translate to a single byte encoding transparently and to maintain backwards compatiblity, obviously. It's also more intuitive for most users because byte strings *seem* like an abstract concept to many, I think.
If the library has a bug -- it should be fixed.
I doubt if exposing subapp prefix in public API is the best way to do it.
Please open a new issue if needed",{},OWNER,User,False

10734,IssueCommentEvent,2018-03-22 07:54:39+00:00,13258039,aio-libs/aiohttp,250019,tarekziade,7049303,aio-libs,307218988,375208905,> a ClientSession != connection, it has a connection pool internally.
This is an implementation detail. In fact, from a user pov, a ClientSession == a connection
> What is a server disconnect? Some exception on requests?
Yes it can be ServerDisconnectedError, but this is not what we want to catch, this is the responsability of the developer.
> If the connection is aborted during a request, usually we cannot retry it automatically, because it might be executed twice on the server side. Users must do this explicitly.
I think you did not understand the use case. It's not a retry. It's the fact that a session will be able to do just a single call and then be closed.

There's a clean close happening from the server *after* the response has been sent. So the idea is not  about retrying a request when it failsm but just checking that the connection is not closed just *before* doing a request with it - and if it's closed, reconnect it.

11025,IssueCommentEvent,2017-05-05 11:26:08+00:00,47576910,googlei18n/fontmake,6939968,anthrotype,5503833,googlei18n,223675922,299441022,"@marekjez86 if you add the new ""Variation Font Origin"" custom parameter to the NotoSerifMyanmar-MM.glyphs font (introduced since Glyphs 2.4.1), and set its value to ""Light"", then you should be able to build the variable font with the latest fontmake 1.2.5 (with [glyphsLib 1.7.3](https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib/releases/tag/v1.7.3)).
I've also sent a PR to noto-souce repo to update the embedded fontmake submodule

The reason varLib fails without that custom parameter in the case of NotoSansMyanmar is that, without that, glyphsLib will fall back to using the master called ""Regular"" as the ""base"" master; this has weight value of 128 (in interpolation space); with that, glyphsLib attempts to find among the instances one whose interpolation weight is the same as the regular master weight value, in order to define the default location for the weight axis, _but_ does not find any in the case of NotoSansMyanmar (there is no instance defined with the same interpolation weight and width as the master called ""Regular"", the closest is the ""SemiBold"" instance which has interpolation weight 130); hence, glyphsLib defaults to 400 as the default (user-space) value for the weight axis; and this is mapped to interpolation weight 94, because the instance called ""Regular"" has weightClass 400 and interpolation weight 94.

Now, varLib takes this designspace and tries to build a variable font; if it finds an axes element in the designspace (this is always the case since glyphsLib 1.7.0), then it uses it to get the default value of each axis (in our case it's 400, mapped to 94); but then it fails because it cannot find any master which has default values along all the axes (i.e. the ""base"" master), since actually none of the NotoSansMyanmar masters happen to have weight 94.

This is a limitation of varLib, which expects that such a base master exists already in the sources (i.e. one whose location along all the axes corresponds to the default locations as defined in the axes element), as it can't currently create one on the fly.

One way to work around this limitation in the case of NotoSerifMyanmar is to add the ""Variation Font Origin"" custom parameter and set it to ""Light"" instead of ""Regular"". If you do that, then glyphsLib will use 100 as the default weight, not 400; and 100 is the weightClass of the ""Thin"" instance, which has the same interpolation weight (28) as the ""Variation Font Origin"" master, called ""Light"".
I know this is very confusing :,(

11218,IssueCommentEvent,2017-04-25 15:17:17+00:00,27609645,web2py/pydal,1295688,abastardi,33118,web2py,223235042,297064082,> - .validate_and_* should NOT ignore writable=False fields. That variable is a pass-through value that should only be used by forms

Just to be clear, I was suggesting ignoring non-writable fields only if they were not explicitly passed in (for example, `compute` fields). And by ""ignore"", I meant don't *validate* default/update values (but still allow the default/update values to be written). In other words, for non-writable fields that are not explicitly included, just allow the usual `.insert` and `.update` behavior (which may result in default/update values, computed values, or null values being inserted, but with no validation happening).
> - it should ONLY validate fields that are passed as input
> - .validate_and_insert should nevertheless loop and insert default values also for fields not passed as input which may have a default value

When you say it should loop and insert default values, do you mean those default values should be validated, or merely inserted? If the latter, there is no need for `.validate_and_insert` to do anything itself, as it ultimately calls `.insert`, which itself will handle inserting any default values for fields that were not explicitly passed in.

The questions are, for fields that are *not explicitly passed in*, (1) which should be validated (all, none, or just the writable ones), and (2) if any are to be validated, what should happen if there is no default/update value to validate (i.e., skip validation, validate `None`, validate an empty string, use different values depending on field type, etc.)?

We must also consider what happens with computed fields (with or without default values). Currently, if a computed field has a default value, its default value will be validated and either get inserted or generate an error -- the computed value will never get computed, even if it could be computed based on the inputs. Furthermore, a computed *string* field with *no default* will always have an empty string inserted rather than its computed value (this is because by default, string fields get a validator that ultimately converts empty/missing values to an empty string).

In answering the above questions, we should be mindful that calling the `.validate_and_` methods with a particular set of inputs may lead to different results from submitting a form with the exact same set of inputs. We should decide how closely we want the `.validate_and_` methods to mimic form validations, as users may be using the same model definitions for both forms and the `.validate_and_` methods, and any inconsistencies could become a subtle source of bugs ([for example](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/web2py/Kqfg-MG5K44/IYX0srNqAgAJ)).

,{},,User,False

11230,IssueCommentEvent,2015-05-03 22:40:31+00:00,4973632,pydata/patsy,609896,njsmith,1284191,pydata,46477477,98552558,Hi @nkconnor, sorry I missed this report initially! Better late than never I guess...

In case you haven't figured it out in the mean time: the problem is that you are not passing any data into your call to `dmatrix`. `dta` is an object that knows how to take a chunk of data and turn it into a chunk of design matrix, but you have to actually pass in the chunk of data. (Note that it's unrealistic to expect to get a single `df` object out which contains your entire design matrix -- your whole problem here is that this object would be too big to fit into memory. So patsy can give you slices of it one at a time, but you still have to figure out how to do something useful with those slices.) You need something like
# ... then do something sensible with dmat_chunk
# This probably requires having logistic regression code that can process design matrices incrementally

Note that this will generally be more efficient if you can arrange for each `data_chunk` to be a sizeable collection set of rows -- there's a fair amount of per-chunk overhead (just in terms of allocating the objects, calling `dmatrix`, etc.), so you want each chunk to be as big as possible while still fitting into memory. (The same applies to the initial call to `incr_dbuilder`.)
Hope that helps! Please re-open if not.",{},,User,False

11672,IssueCommentEvent,2017-12-02 02:02:51+00:00,45906846,jpmml/sklearn2pmml,3462679,vruusmann,3713638,jpmml,275906732,348659432,"> I think the pmmlPipeline does not support a tfidfTokenizer with l2 norm. Is that right?
If the `norm` attribute is anything else than `null` (aka `None`), then the converter should throw the following IllegalArgumentException (sorry, no helpful exception message there):
> Is there a way that I could have a PmmlPipeline with normalized tfidfs?

It is rather difficult to make normalization work, because in order to compute the denominator, one would need to compute term frequencies for all the terms in your vocabulary. However, the PMML representation only keeps ""active"" terms (ie. terms that are actually needed by the model), and omits ""inactive"" terms.

For example, suppose your starting vocabulary contains 10'000 terms, but the random forest model ""uses"" only 1'000 of them. To make normalization work, then it would be necessary to compute the frequencies of the unused 9'000 terms as well - seems like a waste of resources, if they are not needed for anything else at later stages.

Scikit-Learn does not distinguish between ""active"" and ""inactive"" terms so clearly. If there's a `TfIdfVectorizer` transformation in the pipeline, then the frequencies of all 10'000 features would be computed (and it will be straightforward to sum their values to get the denominator value).",{},OWNER,User,False

12066,IssueCommentEvent,2017-07-07 20:43:11+00:00,17689377,pre-commit/pre-commit,1810591,asottile,6943086,pre-commit,241338468,313788236,"I'm going to be honest, I don't really see myself using this feature so I want to have a _really good_ reason for it before implementing.

pre-commit currently doesn't concern itself much with organizing your build and is largely a ""git+filenames => install tools => run tools with filenames"" tool.  It tries to leave your build as open as you want it to be without complicating the primary use case (running during git hooks).
(I may be wrong) but it seems like your current goals are:
- only run a specific list of things during `pre-commit` (maybe all?) (_I'm not even sure this is one of your goals, basing this off of your original post and not your example configuration_)
- In some make targets, run a specific set of hooks against all files

In our projects we've never wanted the first goal (we always want to run all the hooks every time), but it *is* possible using `stages` (simply use `stages: []` for hooks you don't want to run in the `commit` or `push` stages) ([documented here](http://pre-commit.com/#confining-hooks-to-run-at-certain-stages)).  Something like this:
# Only run when explicitly asked for
The second we have done, but we tend to use some other tech (such as a makefile) to group these things, from your example something like this:

12145,IssueCommentEvent,2017-11-27 03:10:45+00:00,91356408,allenai/allennlp,16001974,DeNeutoy,5667695,allenai,275547275,347067908,"I was doing this in response to the feedback on the previous PR, but i'm not particularly happy with how it's turned out, so maybe you're right.

I think the original issue is that if we _aren't_ going to write a `Seq2StackWrapper`, we'll have to duplicate the sorting and packing logic inside the `ElmoLstm`. The problem with writing said wrapper is that really, the class which deals with the sorting and packing really needs to deal with the statefulness, because the states need to be un-sorted and aligned before they are stored, which would make it necessary to also duplicate the statefulness logic into that class as well. Maybe this is a better way forward, because honestly, our library isn't set up to do stateful training for the other encoders, and it's not really like statefulness is something you can ""swap in"" to your model - either it is stateful or it isn't, depending on your data, making the ELMo stuff a special case.
Either way, I think the statefulness is going to be very slow, as with the changes in this PR, we're now doing 4 `index_select`'s per RNN call.

12456,IssueCommentEvent,2016-11-28 05:02:13+00:00,2113510,crossbario/autobahn-python,609896,njsmith,5656948,crossbario,191836357,263184104,"> maybe it's more plausible that some websocket-based protocol would involve the client at some point sending data that originated on the server...

Oh, yeah, duh, actually, that's the attack: a malicious server predicts the next masking key, and then sends a malicious PING with a pre-masked cache-poisoning payload; the returned PONG poisons the middleware cache. AFAICT right now this would actually be a pretty straightforward/practical attack on any autobahn-based client that isn't using TLS and that . The main obstacles I can see would be (1) reversing MT, but that's [fairly well understood](https://github.com/fx5/not_random) and from a quick look at the CPython source it looks like using `getrandbits(32)` actually makes life rather trivial for the attacker, since it seems to directly return the raw, complete MT output. (That link demonstrates an attack on `randrange`, which is more difficult.) And (2) the server only gets to control 125 bytes ... but the [attack payloads here](http://w2spconf.com/2011/papers/websocket.pdf) seem to fit into that easily. They just look like (for example) `""GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: facebook.com

""`, which is 38 bytes.
For what it's worth, it looks like `urandom` can probably be made pretty fast. If pulling 4 bytes at a time, it's substantially slower than `random.getrandbits` (cpython 3.5.2, Linux 4.8.0, x86-64):
But if you fetch multiple masking keys at once, then the amortized cost goes way down:

That's 100 keys in 2.52 us = 1 key in 25 ns (amortized), which is 4x faster than pulling a key at a time out of getrandbits. Of course there's still some overhead I'm not counting for keeping track of the keys in the batch, and a similar batching trick could probably be used for getrandbits, but my point is that it seems plausible that autobahn could switch to `os.urandom` without losing any performance compared to where it currently is. (If mask fetching is even a bottleneck to start with.)",{},,User,False
