12672,IssueCommentEvent,2015-11-04 17:29:41+00:00,22783065,project-generator/project_generator,2963726,0xc0170,11992191,project-generator,114806379,153800851,"The tools specific options are not exposed in yaml.  It is not trivial to provide options in yaml files for many tools. I'll take uvision as an example.

It contain hundreds of options. Their internal xml naming, in many cases is not intuitive (does not match the name in the IDE). We used to cover small subset, but this does not scale. Therefore I implemented templates, where we don't need to learn which options what means, we just need to implement parsing - to inject pgen data in, and all the rest is used from a generic/user's template.
To provide options to yaml files, I can think of 2 options.
1. Use templates (open IDE, set, store), for different options, different templates - in most cases I believe there will be one-two templates. This one is implemented.

2. Add back Misc for some tools, this will require to come up with naming (asm, c/c++, linker flags - and their options). Start with very small subset (I don't have enough man days to cover what 1st can cover). This is a request for pre/post, another project might require for uvision to set uARM vs ARM lib, or IAR another option. with this one, we can easily end up close to 1st, but takes much longer path.

I switched to templates for various reason. I am still considering how to enable yaml settings for those tools , but haven't found yet an easy way to implement and for a user to understand. The easiest is open your tool, select what a project requires, store and use.",{},,User,False

12677,IssueCommentEvent,2017-07-10 19:31:46+00:00,56847043,nfcpy/nfcpy,6604011,nehpetsde,18479744,nfcpy,241703794,314210509,"For NFC tag discovery the reader must generally poll (sense) sequentially for the different technologies (A, B and F). It there is a target in the RF field then a response is received after polling for that technology. If the target supports multiple technologies, as in your case with the smart phone, it is free to choose to which technology to respond, generally this will be the first supported. If the target is brought into range while the reader is polling, the result is naturally random. So strictly speaking it is impossible to have a reader simultaneously detect targets of two different technologies. Though it is possible to implement a different polling strategy where the reader polls until a first response, drops the RF field to reset all targets and then polls for the next technology. Finally the reader can chose the technology at which to activate the target or, if there a re two distinct targets in the field, the one to activate. This kind of loop you'd have to implement directly with the clf.sense methods, something to start with might be the low level polling code in examples/sense.py.",{},,User,False

12713,IssueCommentEvent,2016-02-04 20:28:16+00:00,27790033,blaze/odo,3064397,llllllllll,12833921,blaze,128908874,180034872,The box type is needed because we use the type of objects in the graph. It is sort of unfortunate but luckily it doesn't contribute meaningfully to the runtime. Odo is not designed to be used in an inner loop situation.
You don't need append from sdmx to another type because you have already hooked into the graph by defining the single edge. For example, let's walk through this call:
The first thing odo will do is call `resource` on the strings to figure out what it is working with. This should give us a `PandasSDMX` object and a `sqlalchemy.Table` object respectivly.
Next, odo says, ""is this a conversion or an append"". It sees that the rhs is not a type object but an instance so it knows that this is an append operation.

Then it asks, ""can we append a `PandasSDMX` into an `sa.Table`. Since it cannot do this directly it looks to find a path through the graph. Luckily we have already defined an edge from `PandasSDMX` to `pd.DataFrame` and odo comes with an edge from `pd.DataFrame` to `sa.Table`.
We then perform the needed conversions so that we can append the data.

`discover` should return a datashape object, not a string. This can be parsed with `datashape.dshape`. I am not sure how to get the shape information from one of the response objects you have showed but I imagine there is some way. If not, just convert to a dataframe and then discover the dataframe.

You might be interested in a notebook I did for a short odo tutorial at strata: https://github.com/cpcloud/strata-nyc-2015/blob/master/Extending%20Odo.ipynb. There is no prose as it was live demo; but, I think it will help show an end to end implementation for a trivial type. I will try to pair up some examples and prose from this discussion and turn it into docs for odo.",{},,User,False

12996,IssueCommentEvent,2018-07-17 00:32:27+00:00,93148919,calmjs/calmjs.parse,372914,metatoaster,20507014,calmjs,341689668,405421983,"Ah, what you want is still there, just cast the identifier to `str` (or `unicode` if Python 2) and it should work.  I also took a look at your code and see that a number of changes will be need; while I did say I want to make it a drop-in replacement, it really end up only for the highest level API and when it really was 0.9.0. When I made the 1.0.0 release there were just too many things that needed fixing at the fundamental level I took the liberty to reorganize everything to make it more to my liking.  So that also mean that there's more to this, and it is a good idea to take a look at the [`asttypes`](https://github.com/calmjs/calmjs.parse/blob/1.0.1/src/calmjs/parse/asttypes.py) class definition for the attributes needed, as that will become handy.
Based on the base example on extracting specific nodes [on the readme](https://github.com/calmjs/calmjs.parse#tree-walking), you might want to modify your base class to the following:
# ... skipped the other unchanged methods for the mean time
# using the `u''` to ensure unicode in Python 2
args = node.args.items  # argument lists are now classes
# to simplify following demo, I have this
>>> for node in walker.filter(tree, lambda node: isinstance(node, FunctionCall)):

Another benefit: `ClassDependencies` class for your library is decoupled from any specific framework (now subclassed directly from `object`). Also note using a calling convention with a simple walker like so allows straightforward composition of different things needed to be handled/extracted into the same loop, potentially saving extra ""visitations"" and in my opinion it is a lot less unwieldy than trying to subclass the visitor class (or multiple classes to do two things at once), but now I digress.",{},MEMBER,User,False

13306,IssueCommentEvent,2015-10-21 19:15:24+00:00,25027539,Clinical-Genomics/scout,758570,dnil,6412433,Clinical-Genomics,73630171,149997914,"Ok, have a look at 14:105173862 (INF2) ACCCCACCCCCAC → ACCCCAC
In scout, it gets as cDNA ""HGVS description"":

But, according to HGVS nomenclature, it should be (check with e.g. [Mutalyzer](https://mutalyzer.nl/name-checker?description=NM_022489%3Ac.1259_1270delCCCCACCCCCACinsCCCCAC)) NM_022489(INF2_v001):c.1280_1285del p.(Pro427_Pro428del).

Big deal, its just a poly-proline stretch - hence low prio - but it will make it difficult to find the variant in e.g. article texts. This particular one seems to deviate simply because of the disconnect in VCF norm demanding leftAlign, and hgvs norm being rightAlign.

Looks like the ClinVar entry is from January 2017. Could be a question for MIP / production: how often is the ClinVar reference updated? If so, for Scout: perhaps display references versions somewhere - like an extended delivery report?

13779,IssueCommentEvent,2016-11-18 07:20:55+00:00,985638,django-helpdesk/django-helpdesk,500775,reduxionist,22762644,django-helpdesk,188668540,261465573,"While normalizing with zeroes like that works in the examples used so far, applied to a wider range of creation times (seconds to months is 6 units) I think it gets so wordy that it's not worth using humanization any more. It might look cleaner to just output a standard javascript date type since DataTables can sort those correctly out-of-the-box.

Alternatively, we could use the absolute datetime value (which is stored in the title attribute of the span enclosing the relative value), as an orthogonal data source to sort by as per [this DataTables example](https://datatables.net/manual/data/orthogonal-data#Predefined-values). We'd probably need to adjust the absolute datetime format to be numeric-sort-friendly (e.g. epoch time), or use one of the [DataTables date sorting plugins](https://datatables.net/plug-ins/sorting/#Plug-ins), too.

As a user, sometimes I appreciate relative dates and sometimes I want to know exactly when something occurred and find the coarse-grained months/years -ago format frustrating, so any approach is OK with me really as long it sorts correctly in the end! 😈 ",{},,User,False

13971,IssueCommentEvent,2015-12-17 14:03:50+00:00,14318232,soft-matter/pims,199813,tacaswell,5857177,soft-matter,122153316,165460332,"Ah, I just iced that this PR is from your master branch, git works better if you work on a feature branch.  One of the problems with git is that it is too powerful/flexible and most of the people answering on SO have found _a_ way to do something but it typically is not the right way, but gets upvoted as a pragmatic (wrong) solution.

These two commands rename your remotes because I find 'origin' and 'upstream' super confusing, this is the pattern I use on all of my repos.  One bug/feature of this is that it makes you be explicit about what remote you want to work with as git treats 'origin' as special/automagical.
creates a new feature branch.  `git branch` should now show that you have two branches `master` and `enh_color_norpix` and you are on `enh_color_norpix`.
This will rebase your feature branch on top of current upstream
will push a branch without the merges to you gh repo which you should use to open a PR with
To clean up your master (**WARNING POSSIBLY DESTRUCTIVE**)

which will switch you back to the master branch, forcible reset it to match the soft-matter master branch (that is set the commit that you local branch is pointing to to be the same commit as soft-matter/master is pointing to and discard all local changes in your work tree, hence destructive) and then force push to your public repo to do the same there (hence, destructive).

The thing that caused you problems was the `git pull` which was merging back into your local branch the changes you just worked to remove.  I personally do not use `git pull` as it has done things like this to me a few too many times.  My typical workflow is `git remote update` and then `git merge remote/branch --ff-only`.  If you really want to use pull, I think `git pull --ff-only` does what I expect.
git should ship with a program `gitk` which lets you look at the commit tree, that is an invaluable tool.

I also strongly suggest finding a better front end to git than the command line.  I use `emacs` + `magit` which lets me do most of these things from inside of emacs (which is awesome), but if you do not already use emacs, that can be a steep learning curve.  @danielballan assures me there is an equivalent for `vi` and I have heard good things about the GH gui (but it mostly confused me the one time I tried to use it).
hour read, but helped me understand git: http://tom.preston-werner.com/2009/05/19/the-git-parable.html
very quick read explaining the above workflow with pretty pictures: https://guides.github.com/introduction/flow/",{},,User,False

14122,IssueCommentEvent,2017-06-01 20:04:38+00:00,2014929,scikit-image/scikit-image,6528957,grlee77,897180,scikit-image,61214439,305604972,"> Also the reconstruction is not always exact, it depends on the shape of the image (ideally should be power of two).
Is it really power of two or is it any integer multiple of `2**height`?  (i.e. power of two is only necessary in the case where the maximum possible number of levels of decomposition are performed).

There is currently no check that the user provided `height` is reasonable.  For example, if I change round-trip examples with input of size 128x128 to use an excessive `height=10`, no error is raised, but there is a large error in the round-trip result.  I think we need to raise a `ValueError` if the specified `height` exceeds what is possible for the input array.

If accuracy is likely to be poor for some shapes, perhaps we should raise a warning for the user in those instances?  The user would then know that they may benefit from padding up to an appropriate shape via `np.pad` prior to performing the decomposition.

This was effectively done by https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/1339 . That PR is serializing calls to all filters, which is really coarse, and the logic is not part of the Blockchain proxies, so we may still get races if the proxies are used directly.

Ideally we should lock calls to methods of `Filter` instances, additionally the lock needs to cover the processing of the events. One solution is to wrap the `changes()` function in a context manager, that acquires the locks and return the events, while the events are being processed the lock is held, once the context manager exists the events are cleared. 14370,IssueCommentEvent,2015-07-21 19:21:37+00:00,6235174,conda/conda,293525,ijstokes,6392739,conda,93377736,123452600,"I may be wrong about the exact details, but the basic idea is that `conda update anaconda` will make your current environment match the exact set of packages **plus versions** of the latest Anaconda release.  The latest Anaconda release may have been 5 months ago, and some of the package versions in the **latest Anaconda** are **not** the latest version that is available for the package itself.  The nice thing about `conda update anaconda` is that all the packages will work together (modulo any missed dependency tests, which we do our best to avoid for prior to release), whereas `conda update --all` will try to update *every* package you currently have in your environment in such a way that *all* dependencies are satisfied.  Because of the way `conda` works, this will *normally* be an impossible task if your environment has more than a few dozen packages in it.  Plus even if it is possible the dependency solver may take ~forever to complete.",{},,User,False

14375,IssueCommentEvent,2015-05-06 19:02:34+00:00,6235174,conda/conda,8303684,tanderl,6392739,conda,71914261,99575180,"After a little more spelunking, I determined that in my specific case, conda is determining that it cannot open `C:\Miniconda\Lib/site-packages/Jinja2-2.7.3-py2.7.egg-info` for writing.  However, when I look in my system, that particular path is actually a directory, not a file.  The `C:\Miniconda\Lib\site-packages` directory on my machine has a mixture of entries that end with `.egg-info`.  Some of them are files and some are directories. 14381,IssueCommentEvent,2017-11-03 16:58:27+00:00,6235174,conda/conda,38393,msarahan,6392739,conda,270461076,341764537,"I haven't raised this with appveyor yet.  I don't know what their opinion is.

This problem ultimately originates with an index change we needed to make.  The old ``features`` and ``track_features`` ended up causing some huge problems when more than one package in an environment used ``track_features`` on related ``features``.  For example, python used track_features for a given vc version.  RStudio also used track_features, for a different vc version.  Rather than excluding RStudio from being installed into that python environment, it became ambiguous which feature to pay attention to, and things broke horribly.  Adding the vc package dependency was the answer.

Now as for this error: The vc package that is available has version numbers with no ``.`` in them (9, 10, 14).  I have a theory that ancient conda just doesn't understand that.  The ``.*`` in specs is generally a good idea (it is what lets conda not match exactly), but an exact match here might be all that old conda supports

We're going to try something with the index generation so that hopefully fewer code changes are necessary on your end, but in general, we can't support conda (or python) this old, and Appveyor will need to do something about this.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

14761,IssueCommentEvent,2017-05-10 23:05:35+00:00,1248263,novnc/websockify,17574483,braney,24572588,novnc,221039534,300637162,"I encountered a similar issue (in Linux), but found a solution.  I had the SSL connection working fine with a self-signed certificate, but wanted it to work without having to setup a security exception in the browser.

I obtained a server certificate signed by InCommon.org.  Like you, I tried passing the concatenated file (containing the server certificate + the intermediate CA certificates) via `--cert='file.pem'`, and got the ""no shared cipher"" error.  My command-line was very similar to yours:

I found that it works if the file I specify to `--cert` contains *only* the server certificate, without concatenating the intermediate CA certificates.  My browser already knew of the InCommon CA, so I really didn't need the intermediate CA certificates anyway.  Still, it suggests that passing the concatenated file to websockify is the problem.
If intermediate CA certificates are required to secure your connection, this might be of help:
I found that the call to `ssl.wrap_socket()` in websockifyserver.py could be passed a file containing intermediate CA-certificates file via the `ca_cert` option.",{},,User,False

14823,IssueCommentEvent,2015-08-18 21:11:32+00:00,23924195,Lasagne/Lasagne,897534,Britefury,11752110,Lasagne,100478473,132353159,"@f0k I've fixed the issues you mentioned.

I haven't done any more benchmarking since my last inconclusive benchmarking session, the results of which can be found in this [Google Spreadsheet](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1SnIfxyNv1oeqX1WmhY7SYL8PQL6twA-RPv0Sxue1TmI/edit?usp=sharing). The benchmarking was performed on an nVidia 980. I hope the results are clear. A brief explanation just in case:
- each coloured block represents results, one cell for each scale factor, row by column
- each cell contains thunk time in seconds
- a group of three coloured blocks is top block: forward pass, middle block: backward pass, bottom block: forward + backward pass added together
- the top 3 blocks use `set_subtensor`, the bottom 3 `repeat`.

I am not confident in the quality of the results since they seem susceptible to noise. This is demonstrated by the differences in values for scale factor of e.g. 2x1 and 1x2; they should be very close but often are not. Either the difference in memory striding is making a huge difference or noise is playing a very large factor. Given that results can change if the exact same experiment is run a few times, I think noise is a problem here. I think that in order to get more accurate results, I need to increase the number of repetitions from 1,000 to >10,000. Early tests seem to indicate that this can improve matters.

,{},,User,False

1. When in doubt, do what ""everyone else"" does (i.e. copy behavior from other implementations)
1. If there doesn't seem to be a de facto standard, choose the option that makes the most sense (i.e. what the user would expect to happen)

It seems like we're (incorrectly) requiring a `type` field when we shouldn't (it's not mandatory). We seem to be doing this in order to protect the user from unexpected behavior. That said, the Swagger spec is pretty clear that `type` is taken directly from the JSON Schema spec and follows the same specifications.

I'm not opposed to making the `object` type the default, but I'd like us to do a little more investigation first. For example, all of the examples (that I have seen) from [OpenAPI](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/examples/v2.0/) do specify the type.

More importantly, we might just be mishandling models with a missing type (and thus incorrectly asking authors to specify a type). What happens if we lift the requirement that a type be specified?",{},OWNER,User,False

I’m just going to compare against my own Fraunhofer code, especially for time.
On Nov 9, 2016, at 9:05 AM, Rachel Mandelbaum <notifications@github.com<mailto:notifications@github.com>> wrote:

Thanks to some hints from Josh, I found that 45 of the 60 seconds come from the calculate_maxk step when it initializes the final InterpolatedImage. This would suggest allowing users to pass in a maxk value in cases where they know that they are going to convolve with something else that has a smaller maxk than the optical PSF might have.

So I tried that. Good news: the time to initialize shrank to ~15 seconds as expected. (Next job: figure out what that 15 seconds is.) Bad news: the time to draw the convolution of the optical and atmospheric PSF inflated from well below a second to 40 seconds. That's because the FFT of the InterpolatedImage is only done once, either when you calculate maxK or otherwise, so it has to happen sometime.

Conclusion: we should downsample the file that Aaron sent; perhaps we can get nearly the same results without having ~16 pixels across each spider. I tried downsampling from 2048^2 to 512^2 and even 256^2, in the spirit of living dangerously. 256^2 raised a warning that it wasn't sampled enough even for a simple unobscured Airy, so forget that. 512^2 is nominally fine - at first - and time to initialize drops to 3 seconds from 60 seconds. For reference, the high resolution optical PSF image (2048^2 pupil plane, 0.02 arcsec pixel scale):

I was going to suggest that we go with 512^2 as the default, since it clearly gives almost exactly correct diffraction features much more quickly (and then have a ""highres"" option for those who really care about all the details).
But... when we convolve with a Kolmogorov with FWHM=0.65 arcsec and draw into an image with 0.2 arcsec pixels, something funny happens. After using 2048^2 pupil plane image:

You see a funny box feature. The origin of the feature is that when we use the lower res pupil plane image, that sets a maximum physical extent of the interpolated image stored inside the OpticalPSF - 3 arcsec or so. But if you are convolving with a reasonable atmospheric PSF, the natural image size is larger than that. So you get a funny box feature that is not easily removed by changing the OpticalPSF kwargs like gsparams, oversampling, or pad factor.
Then I came up with plan B:

If the high-res optical PSF is very expensive to initialize but not nearly as expensive to use, we could cache the results of the initialization step in share/ and then the LSST PSF routine could use the cached things (unless different oversampling etc. are requested).
That also doesn't work for two reasons:
1 - the stuff to be cached would also depend on the aberrations, which presumably will differ for different calls
2 - Josh pointed out that the stuff to be cached depends on wavelength
I'm stumped. Anyone else have suggestions?

15148,IssueCommentEvent,2018-05-07 10:35:49+00:00,31315021,Kinto/kinto,546692,leplatrem,13413813,Kinto,320450016,387026133,> It isn't really clear what the scalability characteristics of Kinto are or should be. Is Kinto meant to store 10m records? I would say probably not. What about 1m records, but all in one collection? I'm not certain.
10m records accross several collections, I would say yes.
1m records in one collection, it should support it, but maybe not in optimal conditions. Since synchronization is going to be painful anyway (especially on the first time).

> It isn't really clear (to me, at least) what queries Kinto should support as ""first-class"" and which are ""gravy"", i.e. added bonuses that we threw in because they were easy to implement but are not as well-loved, performant etc. as others. Naïvely I would say syncing collections is more important than supporting arbitrary filters, but then again, if that's what you want, CouchDB is available and may be a better fit for you.
I definitely agree that syncing is more important than arbitrary filters (for which we have the ElasticSearch plugin)

> It isn't really clear to me what the relative priorities are in the Kinto ecosystem. As an example, in order to produce a Total-Records header, we have to add a COUNT to our queries, which means enumerating all the matching records rather than just the ones that we're going to render in the response. In other distributed systems, you might just paginate until you get an empty page. Is the ease-of-use/sanity checking provided by the Total-Records header worth the performance cost?
That was a naive choice at the beginning of the project. The `Total-Records` is not even exposed in our clients API...
On the server side, the total count is just used to figure out whether we have a next page. Which, as you say, could be managed differently (ie. paginate until empty page).

How would I do this? In a text based format with a git backend I would select ""edit entry"", commit the change directly or via a pull request, and the responsible person could merge the change.
How would this work in the case of HumanCyc?

How are you tracking changes to the entries in the database? Is there a complete log of changes by whom (for attribution)? Both the changing of information and attribution are ""for free"" with a git based system.

> Just as to update a shared git repository, a user has to be granted access to the repository.  So one could just as well accuse a git-based project of ""hiding information in a private repository"".
This is not true. Everybody can make a pull request, no login, user data, account or anything needed. You can promote users to trusted commiters, but everybody can propose changes in a simple manner.
> The XML-based formats used by many model developers are not easy to update and are not at all git-friendly.
I completely agree. They are good as exchange formats, but not suited to track changes.
> I really hope the community is working into this direction instead of hiding information in in-house databases.
Yes, this happens independent of the the used technology, but is dependent on the license of the data. There are open databases like Reactome (CCO) and there are private repositories and vica versa.

15346,IssueCommentEvent,2017-04-20 18:40:57+00:00,55808787,openthread/openthread,686678,darconeous,16142269,openthread,222579435,295851557,"Technically, the `--with-*`/`--without-*` options are supposed to be used for determining which external dependencies to use. It is the `--enable-*`/`--disable-*` that is supposed to be used for adjusting features.
> There are three types of options (or properly arguments ) that can be added to the configure script:

>     The arguments starting with `--enable-` prefix are usually used to enable features of the program. They usually add or remove dependencies only if they are needed for that particular feature being enabled or disabled.
>    The arguments starting with `--with-` prefix are usually used to add or remove dependencies on external projects. These might add or remove features from the project.
>    Environment variables that are used by the configure script should also be declared as arguments; their use will be explained below in detail.

> The first two kinds of parameters differ just for the displayed name and from the macro used, but are in effect handled mostly in the same way. They both are actually used to pass variables, in the form of `--(enable|with)-foo=bar` and both provide defaults for when the variable is omitted (the value yes for `--enable` and `--with` and the value no for `--disable` and `--without`).

> While there is no technical difference between the two, it's helpful for both users and distribution to follow the indications given above about the use of the two parameters' kind. This allows to identify exactly what the parameters are used for.
Using the command-line environment variable to specify the bus type seems like the most natural way to go about this, but I'm fairly flexible.",{},,User,False

16267,IssueCommentEvent,2017-05-31 22:15:18+00:00,90762745,py-pa/django-minio-storage,330847,thomasf,29058518,py-pa,232432297,305333550,I want to add a couple of more implementations in the future.

The first one (besides the spooledtempfile) is a version which just wraps the stream directly from the minio object because there are situations where the raw stream also is useful even though it means that there will be no support for seeking..
The second one is a version which actually implements seek and chunks directly via the minio object which also can be useful even though it can potentially be alot slower.

These two versions should not be default, imho they have more hidden costs and quirks than the spooled tempfile solution so users should probably be forced to instantiate those storages manually in their django projects.
I don't think I will implement additional classes in this pull request though so I'll remove the parent class until it actually makes sense to have it.",{},,User,False

16639,IssueCommentEvent,2015-11-28 17:25:06+00:00,2504175,nodeca/js-yaml,1039540,dervus,723678,nodeca,118845464,160322223,"I fear you're turning skipSeparationSpace function into a bigger mess than it already is. The point is — JS-YAML strictly follows (at least tries to) formal grammar definition. Not general statements in the spec. Only formal grammar definition. In this situation we must be able to put a comment at every line of code with a reference to grammar rule in the spec. The problem with skipSeparationSpace is (AFAIK) that it tries to combine different but similar rules under one straight-forward block of code. It was a mistake because it seems like I accidentally mixed two distinct rule into one. These rules as I stated in the very first comment on #80 are:

> [s-l-comments](http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#s-l-comments) grammar rule is missed, and [s-separate](http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#s-separate%28n,c%29) is used instead: https://github.com/nodeca/js-yaml/blob/454c7e780cb7b3f9c2fc9f9e03fac1c08c1f2d01/lib/js-yaml/loader.js#L1036

As I also stated there — it's a complicated problem, and I can't just take a look at your solution and say: ""Yeah, that's correct"". @ingydotnet's words about tabs can't change this either. Yes, I know that tabs are considered as content in YAML, not as indentation. But it is a general statement, and we must follow only grammar rules in the spec. These rules don't say just ""Hey, no tabs allowed"" as you code assumes if I understood it correctly.

So, what I trying to say is that skipSeparationSpace is messed up in the first place. It implements few rules, and I think nobody can say right now which rules. In this situation I just can't accept any change there without references to rules in the spec. The best solution would be to decompose this function instead to trying to add another hack into it.
By the way, there also should be this test sample:

Pusher libraries are split in to two types: http libraries for triggering, authenticating private channels, webhooks etc. and websocket libraries for subscribing to channels and listening to events. There are no current plans for an official python websocket library, or to build that functionality in to this library, though people have certainly had some success with community libraries and/or writing their own client that implements the [Pusher websocket protocol](https://pusher.com/docs/pusher_protocol). A list of official libraries can be found [here](https://pusher.com/docs/libraries).",{},,User,False

The bfloat16 is a truncated float32 which maintains the dynamic range of the float32 (~1e-38 to ~3e38.) while reducing the significand's precision.

The gain is to avoid the overflow/underflow that a standard float16 would hit due to its limited range (~6e-8 to 65504). In a float16, an exploding gradient can very easily hit the 66k limit of a float16. This otherwise is rather tricky for a normal coder to avoid in the float16, but easily handled in the bfloat16's larger range. Tensorflow's ops normally use bfloat16 in mixed precision by doing multiplies in bfloat16 and summation into float32.
Conversion to/from float32 to bfloat16 is quite trivial as seen in Tensorflow's [bfloat16 code](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/core/framework/bfloat16.cc).

The main limiting factor for why to not support bfloat16 in ONNX is the lack of current hardware support. Only Google's TPUs, and various upcoming Intel processors support bfloat16, which limits the number of models which will contain bfloat16. Whereas IEEE float16 has wide support in GPUs.",{},NONE,User,False

17208,IssueCommentEvent,2018-04-17 01:53:58+00:00,35531022,opendatacube/datacube-core,1428024,Kirill888,26125288,opendatacube,313197634,381804450,"After some discussions there is an extra constraint to consider:

- Stacking process does not create new datasets and does not modify existing dataset metadata documents, instead it just changes dataset location (stored in a separate table) by adding new dataset location with a path of  a stacked netcdf file and marking previous non-stacked location as ""archived"".

This means that an ""obvious solution"" of adding extra field to the metadata document of the dataset to indicate which slice of a stacked netcdf file it occupies is not ideal, as it does not support ""stacking as a location change"" mode of operation of the current stacker.

There seems to be an agreement that current ""fuzzy matching"" approach needs fixing. Location of a dataset within a stacked netcdf should be clearly defined as an index. Even if we replace ""fuzzy matching"" with ""exact matching"", there is still a problem of:
- What happens when timestamp is missing?
- What happens when there are duplicate timestamps in the file?
- Need to have file open before knowing what parts of the file to read

1. Add optional `metadata` column to `dataset_location` table (`json` format), one could then store driver specific data there, time index would then go there as well as other data like dimensions/chunking. Requires schema migration and code change.

2. Add ""fragment"" part to the file uri with driver specific data, example `file:///stacked.nc#tidx=20`, it's then up to the driver to interpret the part following `#`. This approach doesn't require schema migration and can be made backward compatible. Just need to be careful in the code that maps URI to a file, need to make sure that fragment part is discarded.

I personally think that approach in (2.) is quite reasonable. It gives clear indication that only part of the file is ""claimed"" by a given dataset, it does not require db migration and a tool to update locations for existing stacked datasets can be easily implemented.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

Perhaps instead of replacing our use of `pip install` we should consider the `pip wheel` command.. It offers the same Index options as `install` (such as `--find-links`, `--no-index`, etc) which are vital to our internal use, while also fetching wheels and building them when they are not already available.

Then, to build a multi-platform pyz, you'd simply need to _download_ the wheels using  `pip` itself, via the `download` args you gave as an example ( `--python-version 36 --platform manylinux1_x86_64 --abi cp36m --implementation cp --only-binary=:all: Cython numpy` ), and a 2nd step (building the pyz via shiv) using `-f /path/to/wheelhouse --no-index`

Sure, it's an extra step, which can complicate build scripts (but only slightly), but it allows us to retain the important behavior of packaging a single site-packages directory (vital for pkg_resources performance) while also preserving the original API.
I am still concerned about `.pth` files being missing from packages that are installed via wheel. Haven't had a chance to dig into that or investigate how `nspkg.pth` files are handled.

17848,IssueCommentEvent,2015-04-30 16:50:35+00:00,2562442,mozilla-services/cornice,879400,ktomala,1066228,mozilla-services,71611023,97876721,"Perhaps in the user-friends use case it may not be as obvious as to what the advantages are, but assume for a second that you have an interface tailored to the data store. This interface based on Cornice Resource has built-in methods for automatic querying specific objects from the data store that are dependent upon URI path. So when you query ""/user/{id}"" you get User object from the data store based upon it's id. If the interface is built on the principle that it automatically queries specified objects and you have sub-resource that is related to the parent object, i.e. Friend, the interface knows that it has to first query User (i.e. make checks for it's id, perform data store fetch, transform data, etc.) and then use this User object as an input to search for specific relation named ""friends"" and query the data store for this object. However you can imagine that you want to first check for User object existence in the data store (essentially duplicating the code in the /user/{id} GET method), before you query for specific Friend. This interface will then perform the parent object check, query and transformation transparently for you, without code duplication. This is where I think it is useful to have it in one class.

Bear in mind that what I have stated as an example, should be treated as one - an example. Maybe not very useful or self-explanatory. Of course. You can create another class inherited from Resource that implements this functionality, or you can create many different resources like you have shown before, but then you loose coherence and it may not be as straightforward or even possible to use an interface like I have mentioned above. Of course that is not a problem if this commit gets rejected from Cornice, because I can simply create another resource-like class for my needs, that will implement this functionality and work properly with this interface.

However, there's another use case, which may seem weak, but not every RESTful interface is pretty or even designed properly according to principles. Let's suppose you have a legacy interface, that is ported to Cornice and the way the interface was designed is that it has some RPC-like methods on a specific resource. This is only an example, but sometimes you have to define additional methods that are bound to the resource state (during a transaction or another kind of process) like for example /users/{id}/activate or perhaps /vm/{id}/shutdown, etc.

You could use a Service for it, but IMO that looks messy and may be easily omitted by people reading the code. Also there's a problem with resource state dependent actions, that you'd have to create a state container shared between the resource and service, which again looks messy, introduces sometimes unnecessary abstraction layer and may result in performance impact. Of course you can decorate your resource class methods with Service decorators instantiated in __init__ method of the resource, but this looks messy and inconsistent.
However if you opt to reject this commit, that should not be a problem for me, because I will simply reimplement this functionality in an inherited class.",{},,User,False

We could allow for an optional `--plugins-dir` argument to change where Certbot looks for plugin scripts, with a relative path as the default.

> We'd have to be pretty careful with the permissions on the plugin directories. Since `certbot-auto` uses `sudo` a lot internally and is often run from things like cron/systemd where it may already have root privileges, having sane permissions on these directories and files is very important.

We could follow the convention we established for credentials files and check permissions early, printing warnings as necessary. This should help ensure that users are alerted to the issue when they're first configuring things.

> For the purposes of brainstorming, alternatives to scripts could be just a list of python python packages or a pip requirements file. The downside of these approaches is it may not allow hash checking or installation of non-Python dependencies. We could also have users places these things at a predefined location, allow a flag for it to be configurable, or even have the user modify `certbot-auto` itself if it's something simple like a list of packages.

In addition to the other disadvantages, these also seem to require more design and more work. (Running a directory of scripts is an established pattern and may be as simple as invoking `run-parts` with the right arguments, if it's available on all of the systems we care about.)",{},,User,False
I've been running the same issue, and i found this tread by ""googleing"".
So, besides this issue is duplicated and closed already, i just want to share my solution that worked for me:
I could reproduce the issue because, LLC was not set on my remote server, so whenever i connected through SSH i saw this at console:
manpath: can't set the locale; make sure $LC_* and $LANG are correct
and in file /etc/default/locale add line LC_ALL=""en_US.UTF-8"" and relogin to you server
After this, finish and start your ssh session again. Ensure that warning log is gone.
Now `sudo certbot --nginx` is working fine form me.
I hope this does help to others as well.",{},NONE,User,False

18028,IssueCommentEvent,2015-02-12 17:42:54+00:00,15169689,vmware/pyvmomi,33919,hartsock,473334,vmware,57403952,74116304,I'm actually in the process of discussing this with various project owners. This project is a [VMware Labs fling](https://labs.vmware.com/about) and does not fall under the normal support and release cycle umbrella. We release asynchronously and we're not under the same constraints as other projects (we're under different constraints in resources, legality, and process).

At the moment there is no relationship at all between the two SDK. No one on this project was consulted in any way on the development of any other SDK that VMware is presently releasing. We have no meaningful input into that process at all. That said, I would *like* to pursue as *seamless* a developer experience between the various SDK as possible.

It would be beneficial for us to determine the best way to do this with the least effort from either party. As a personal aspirational statement, I would like to see this manner of development (as seen on pyVmomi) become the de facto standard method for VMware to pursue enhancement and development of SDK. This is a naturally controversial statement and may not reflect the larger considerations that VMware as a corporate entity may choose to pursue.

As a general rule, because of this projects status within the VMware ecosystem you can expect it to lag the official release cycle by roughly 6 months and experience a quiet period during Q4 and Q1 of each year... some quiet periods just ahead of and just after official releases... and then hopefully a flurry of activity after we hear experimental API and changes are now ready for public consumption.",{},,User,False

I think I understand why the crash happens, but I am not sure yet how to fix it in ``typing_extensions``. The problem is that old version of ``typing`` calls ``issubclass(cls, self.__extra__)`` even if the latter is an ABC that will return to the same class via ``__subclasses__``. This can be (and already is) fixed in recent ``typing``.

A possible solution for ``typing_extensions`` is to define a custom metaclass inheriting from ``GenericMeta`` that will override ``__subclasscheck__``. This could fix the issue, @jabdoa2 could you please open an issue on https://github.com/python/typing/issues?",{},NONE,User,False

18367,IssueCommentEvent,2016-11-29 20:49:03+00:00,6930489,facebook/watchman,117777,wez,69631,facebook,191810610,263694232,"It's been a little while since I looked at symlink handling on windows.  From what I recall, I chose to skip it in the current windows implementation because they required special privileges to create and that didn't seem to match up with the target use case: repos managed by non-privileged users.

Adding more awareness of windows symlinks means more formally moving away from the MSVCRT functions for working with the file system and (more importantly in the context of this discussion) refactoring watchman to not assume that it has POSIX filesystem APIs available.

That is something that will soon become more feasible; I have a stack of diffs that is starting to lead in that direction, but it is not a current goal among the folks working on Watchman at FB.  We will happily accept contributions that help us get there; we just aren't focusing on that ourselves right now.

18623,IssueCommentEvent,2016-06-01 18:55:28+00:00,33884891,apache/incubator-airflow,2229094,PeterAttardo,47359,apache,157761435,223090499,"I considered the issue to be two related issues:
1. For any task B, which depends on task A (marked as only_run_latest), the dependencies for an instance of B should be met if any instance of A has succeeded on or after the execution date.
2. Avoid scheduling multiple instances of a task that has been marked as only_run_latest and prioritize the most recent execution date.

This PR only addresses the first of those. The second piece raised many more architectural questions and potential changes to the way Airflow schedules jobs. I didn't want to commit to a potentially large architectural change before seeing how the maintainers were thinking about designing for this feature.",{},,User,False

18704,IssueCommentEvent,2016-03-21 13:42:23+00:00,52452814,bitcraze/crazyflie-lib-python,1464817,krichardsson,5947135,bitcraze,140062907,199284433,"Again, I don't really think there is an either or, why not both?
I like your point that travis can test on multiple OSes, even though its not part of the config yet. That makes it worth using the ""native"" travis config for me.

I still think it is valuable to be able to run static code analyses, tests and so on in a docker container for the users that like that solution. My thinking is that it would run the checks in one environment only, the one set up in the docker image. What is needed for that is
1. A bash or python script in the tools/build dir that calls the appropriate tools to run tests and so on
1. The tools that are used by the above script must be added in the docker builder image, if missing (defined in https://github.com/bitcraze/docker-builder)

1. If possible, let travis run the tests in the docker container as well as a part of the build job. This would ensure that the docker images is kept in sync over time. I'm not sure if this can be done in the same job, but I think it is possible.
